name: Vegan Brownies
description: Dense, fudgy brownies with a crackly top — completely plant-based and impossible to tell apart from the original
notes: The espresso powder is optional but amplifies the chocolate without making it taste of coffee. Walnuts are optional; swap for pecans, or leave them out entirely. Make sure the brownies are completely cool before slicing — they firm up as they cool and will slice cleanly.

Pantry.text: 1/2 tbsp espresso powder (optional; or instant coffee)

Condiments.text: 100 ml vegetable oil (e.g. rapeseed, sunflower, canola)

steps.text: Preheat the oven to 180°C (160°C fan). Line a 20×20 cm baking tin with baking paper.
Grind {flax seed} to a fine powder using a blender or spice grinder. Whisk with 75 ml of the {water} in a small bowl and set aside for five minutes to gel into a flax egg.
Combine {sugar} and the remaining 120 ml of water in a small saucepan over medium heat. Stir for six to eight minutes until the sugar fully dissolves — do not let it boil. Remove from heat.
Whisk {plain flour}, {cocoa powder}, {icing sugar}, and {espresso powder} (if using) together with a pinch of salt in a large bowl.
Add the warm sugar syrup, {vegetable oil}, {vanilla extract}, and the flax egg to the dry ingredients. Fold with a spatula until just combined — do not overmix. The batter will be thick and fudgy.
Fold in {walnut} and 100 g of the {chocolate chip}. Pour into the prepared tin and scatter the remaining 30 g chocolate chips on top.
Bake for thirty-five to forty minutes. A skewer may not come out clean — that's intentional, these are meant to be fudgy.
Leave to cool completely in the tin before slicing into 16 pieces. For firmer brownies, refrigerate once cooled.

serving_suggestions: Best at room temperature or slightly warm. Store in an airtight container for up to three days, or refrigerate for up to a week.

name: Black Pepper Tofu
description: Crispy fried tofu smothered in a rich, buttery sauce of shallots, garlic, chillies, and an unapologetic amount of black pepper.
notes: Use firm or extra-firm tofu — it needs to hold up to frying. Press it dry for at least twenty minutes if you have time. The 5 tbsp of coarsely ground black pepper is not a typo; it is the point of the dish. Grind it yourself for the best texture and flavour.

Fresh.text: 16 small spring onions, cut into 3 cm pieces

Fridge.text: 800 g tofu, firm, pressed and cut into 3 cm cubes
Fridge.preparation: firm, pressed and cut into 3 cm cubes

steps.text: Toss {tofu} cubes with {corn starch} until evenly coated. Heat {vegetable oil} in a large frying pan or wok over medium-high heat. Fry the tofu in batches until golden and crisp on all sides, about three minutes per batch. Drain on paper towels and set aside. Discard the oil and wipe the pan clean.

Return the pan to medium heat and melt {vegan butter}. Add {shallots}, {garlic}, {ginger}, and {red chillies}. Cook, stirring occasionally, until completely softened and starting to colour, about fifteen minutes.
Stir in {black pepper}, {kecap manis}, {soy sauce}, {dark soy sauce}, and {sugar}.
Return the tofu to the pan and fold gently to coat. Cook for two minutes until warmed through.
Stir in {spring onions} and serve immediately.

serving_suggestions: Serve over steamed jasmine or basmati rice. A side of steamed pak choi or a simple cucumber salad balances the heat well.

name: Crushed Puy Lentils with Tahini and Cumin
description: Puy lentils crushed into a thick, spiced base of garlic, cumin, and fresh tomato, finished with tahini and lemon.

Fresh.text: 3 medium tomatoes, skinned and cut into 1 cm dice
Fresh.preparation: skinned and cut into 1 cm dice

Fresh.text: 25 g coriander, leaves only, chopped, divided
1/2 small red onion, very thinly sliced

Condiments.text: 2 tbsp olive oil, plus extra to finish

steps.text: Bring a pan of water to a boil. Add {puy lentils} and cook for fifteen to twenty minutes until completely tender. Drain and set aside.

Melt {vegan butter} with {olive oil} in a large sauté pan over medium-high heat. Add {garlic} and {ground cumin} and cook for one minute. Add {tomatoes}, most of the {coriander}, and the cooked lentils. Stir and cook for two minutes.

Add {tahini}, {lemon juice}, {water}, {salt}, and a generous grind of pepper. Reduce the heat to medium and cook gently, stirring, for a few minutes until hot and thickened. Roughly mash with a potato masher until some lentils are broken up and the mixture has a thick, porridge-like consistency.
Spread on a flat platter and run a fork through to create a wavy pattern. Scatter over the remaining {coriander} and {red onion}, and finish with a drizzle of olive oil. Serve warm.

serving_suggestions: Good with warm flatbread or pitta.

name: Grilled Carrots and Tofu with Harissa
description: Charred tofu and baby carrots smothered in a fragrant harissa and ginger sauce, served over a smooth silken tofu and tahini cream.
notes: You need two pans running at once towards the end — read through the method before you start. The silken tofu cream can be made ahead and refrigerated.

Fresh.text: 3 spring onions, whites finely chopped, green tops julienned and soaked in cold water
Fresh.preparation: whites finely chopped, green tops julienned and soaked in cold water

Fridge.text: 280 g tofu, extra-firm, drained and patted dry

steps.text: Blitz {silken tofu}, {tahini}, and a pinch of salt in a food processor until completely smooth. Set aside.

Put {rose harissa}, {maple syrup}, {apple cider vinegar}, {soy sauce}, {garlic}, {ginger}, the chopped {spring onion} whites, and 1 tbsp {olive oil} in a medium sauté pan over medium-high heat. Cook for five minutes, stirring, until the oil splits and the sauce is fragrant and slightly reduced.

Heat a griddle pan over medium-high heat and ventilate the kitchen. Cut the firm {tofu} lengthwise into 6 slices, then halve each piece lengthways to give 12 slices. Grill in two batches for one minute per side until charred, transferring each batch to the sauce pan as it's done. Once all the tofu is in, stir in {cold water} and simmer for five minutes.

Toss the {baby carrots} with the remaining 1/2 tsp {olive oil} and a pinch of salt. Grill on the same griddle, turning as needed, for five to ten minutes until charred and tender. Gently stir into the tofu pan to coat in the sauce.
Spread the tofu cream over a serving platter. Arrange the tofu and carrots on top. Drain the spring onion greens and scatter over. Squeeze {lime} over the top and serve immediately.

serving_suggestions: Serve with flatbread or crusty bread to scoop up the tofu cream.

name: Noor's Lime Tofu
description: Crispy deep-fried tofu simmered in a rich, cumin-spiced tomato sauce with wilted spinach and quick-pickled red onion.

Fresh.text: 1 small red onion (60 g), thinly sliced into rounds
2 limes, juice and zest of (about 1 tbsp each)
Fresh.preparation: juice and zest of (about 1 tbsp each)

Fridge.text: 560 g tofu, extra-firm, patted dry and cut into 2 cm cubes
Fridge.preparation: extra-firm, patted dry and cut into 2 cm cubes

steps.text: Mix {apple cider vinegar}, half the {caster sugar}, {red onion}, and a pinch of salt in a small bowl. Set aside to pickle while you prepare the rest.

Heat {vegetable oil} in a medium high-sided pan over medium-high heat. Toss {tofu} with {corn starch} until well coated. Fry in two batches until crispy and golden, about six minutes per batch. Drain on paper towels and set aside.

Pulse {onions} and {garlic} in a food processor until very finely minced but not puréed. Heat {olive oil} in a large sauté pan over medium-high heat. Cook the onion mixture, stirring occasionally, until softened and lightly browned, about ten minutes.

Add {cumin seeds}, {lime} juice and zest, and {tomato paste}. Cook for one minute. Add {water}, the remaining {caster sugar}, {salt}, and a generous grind of pepper. Simmer for six minutes, stirring occasionally, until thick and rich.
Stir in the fried tofu and {parsley} and another grind of pepper. Add {spinach} in increments, stirring until just wilted, about three minutes.
Transfer to a shallow serving platter and top with the pickled {red onion}.

serving_suggestions: Serve with flatbread or rice to soak up the sauce.

name: Puy Lentil and Aubergine Stew
description: Tender aubergine and puy lentils simmered in a rich tomato and herb broth, served with a dollop of vegan yogurt and chilli flakes.

Fresh.text: 1 large red onion (160 g), finely chopped
2 small aubergines (420 g), cut into 5 x 2 cm chunks
Fresh.preparation: (420 g), cut into 5 x 2 cm chunks

Condiments.text: 3 tbsp olive oil, plus extra to serve, divided

steps.text: Heat 2/3 of the {olive oil} in a large high-sided sauté pan over medium-high heat. Add {garlic}, {red onion}, {thyme}, and a fifth of the {salt}. Fry for eight minutes, stirring often, until soft and golden. Tip into a bowl, leaving the oil behind.

Add the remaining {olive oil} to the same pan and heat until very hot. Season {aubergines} and {cherry tomatoes} with another fifth of the {salt} and plenty of pepper, then add to the pan. Fry for ten minutes over medium-high heat, turning often, until the aubergine is soft and golden-brown and the tomatoes are beginning to blacken.

Return the onion and garlic to the pan. Add {puy lentils}, {vegetable stock}, {water}, and the remaining {salt}. Bring to the boil, then reduce the heat to medium and simmer gently for about forty minutes, until the lentils are soft but still retain some bite.
Serve warm or at room temperature, topped with a dollop of {vegan yogurt}, a drizzle of olive oil, {chilli flakes}, and {oregano}.

name: Vegan Shakshuka
description: Silken tofu seasoned with kala namak to mimic eggs, poached in a rich, spiced tomato sauce.
notes: Kala namak (black salt) has a high sulphur content that gives tofu a remarkably egg-like flavour and aroma. Find it at Indian or Asian grocery stores. Don't skip it — it's what makes this dish work.

Fridge.text: 175 g silken tofu, drained and cut into cubes

steps.text: Heat {olive oil} in a large pan over low-medium heat. Add {onion} and fry until soft and translucent, about eight minutes, stirring frequently. Add {garlic} and fry for two more minutes.
If using an {aubergine}, push the onion mixture to the side and fry the aubergine separately in a little oil until golden, about eight minutes, then combine.
Add {red pepper} and fry until soft, about five minutes. Stir in {ground cumin}, {smoked paprika}, and {chilli powder} and fry for one minute, stirring constantly.

Add {tomato paste}, then {chopped tomatoes} and {water}. Season with {salt}. Simmer over low-medium heat for forty-five minutes, stirring occasionally, until the sauce is thick and rich. Taste and adjust with salt and {brown sugar} if needed.

Toss {silken tofu} cubes with {kala namak} and {turmeric}. Nestle into the sauce along with {frozen peas} if using. Cover and heat on low for a few minutes until the tofu is warmed through and the peas have thawed.
Serve straight from the pan, scattered with {parsley} and a drizzle of {chilli oil}.

serving_suggestions: Serve with crusty bread or pitta to scoop up the sauce.

name: Life-Changing Tofu
description: Tofu coated in potato starch, fried until crispy, then glazed in a sweet-savory ginger and garlic sauce
notes: This technique comes from Chinese and Korean cooking. The potato starch coating crisps up beautifully without absorbing much oil. Fresh ginger is strongly preferred here — grate it on the fine side of a box grater. The tofu keeps well and is excellent cold the next day in a lunchbox.

Condiments.text: 3 tbsp vegetable oil (e.g. rapeseed, sunflower, canola)

steps.text: Place {tofu} cubes in a bag or bowl, add {potato starch}, and toss until every piece is evenly coated.

Heat {vegetable oil} in a large frying pan over high heat until shimmering. Add the tofu in a single layer and fry for three to four minutes per side until the coating is golden and crispy. Transfer to a plate lined with kitchen paper.
While the tofu fries, combine {soy sauce}, {agave syrup}, and {water} in a small bowl. Grate in {ginger} and {garlic} and stir well.
Pour the sauce into the same pan (or a smaller one) and bring to a vigorous boil over high heat. Add the tofu and toss until completely coated and the sauce has reduced slightly.
Season with black pepper and serve immediately.

serving_suggestions: Serve over steamed rice or rice noodles with broccoli or kimchi on the side. Also excellent wrapped in spring rolls.

name: Cauliflower & Pea Korma
description: Creamy, fragrant korma with tender cauliflower florets and sweet peas in a rich coconut tomato sauce
notes: You can substitute broccoli for the cauliflower if you prefer. Remove the chilli seeds for a milder dish.

Fresh.text: 1 head cauliflower (about 600 g), cut into small florets
1 large red pepper, deseeded and finely chopped

Condiments.text: 1 1/2 tbsp vegetable oil (e.g. rapeseed, sunflower, canola)

steps.text: Place {frozen peas} in a bowl of boiling water to thaw.
Heat {vegetable oil} in a large pan over high heat. Once hot, add {garlic}, {ginger}, {red chilli} and {spring onions}. Cook for one and a half minutes, stirring regularly.

durations.text: for one and a half minutes

steps.text: Add {cauliflower} florets, {red pepper} and {cumin seeds}. Cook for two minutes, stirring regularly.
Drain the peas. Add the peas along with {coconut milk}, {chopped tomatoes}, {curry powder}, {black pepper}, {salt} and {maple syrup}. Bring to the boil, then reduce to a simmer for two minutes.
Remove from the heat and stir in the {lime} juice.
Serve topped with {coriander}, {flaked almonds} and {chilli flakes}.

serving_suggestions: Serve with basmati or brown rice and warm naan bread.

name: Express Tantanmen Ramen
description: Quick Japanese ramen with a rich peanut-tahini broth, crispy smoked tofu crumbles and soy-marinated eggs
notes: Start the marinated eggs the evening before — they need at least one night in the fridge. Everything else comes together in about thirty-five minutes. The nutty base and tofu can be prepped simultaneously while the broth heats.

Fridge.text: 300 ml oat milk or soy milk

Condiments.text: 12 tbsp soy sauce (6 for marinade, 2 each for broth, tofu and nutty base)
Condiments.preparation: (6 for marinade, 2 each for broth, tofu and nutty base)

Condiments.text: 3 tsp crispy chili oil (1 for tofu, 2 for nutty base)

steps.text: One day ahead — marinate the eggs: Soft-boil the {eggs} in salted water for six to seven minutes, then transfer to cold water and peel. Mix 150 ml {cold water}, 6 tbsp {soy sauce}, {sugar} and {rice vinegar} in a jar. Submerge the eggs, seal and refrigerate overnight.

Crispy tofu: Pat the {smoked tofu} dry and crumble into small pieces. Heat {sesame oil} in a pan over medium-high heat. Add the tofu with 2 tbsp {soy sauce}, {ground ginger} and 1 tsp {crispy chili oil}. Fry for fifteen to twenty minutes, stirring occasionally, until golden and crispy. Set aside.
Nutty base: Whisk together {peanut butter}, {tahini}, 2 tbsp {soy sauce} and 2 tsp {crispy chili oil}. Thin with a small splash of water until pourable. Set aside.
Broth and noodles: Bring {vegetable stock} and {oat milk} to a gentle simmer with 2 tbsp {soy sauce}. Cook the {ramen noodles} separately according to the packet instructions.

Assemble: Spoon the nutty base into two bowls. Pour the hot broth over and stir to combine. Divide the noodles between the bowls, then top with the crispy tofu, {nori}, {chives} and {sesame seeds}. Halve the marinated eggs and nestle them in. Season with {salt} to taste.

serving_suggestions: Serve immediately while piping hot. Add extra crispy chili oil on top for more heat.

name: Indonesian Satay
description: Rich and nutty peanut sauce with chickpeas and crunchy vegetables, ready in 15 minutes
notes: Use tamari instead of soy sauce to make this gluten-free. You can swap the courgette and red pepper for any fast-cooking veg — mushrooms, sugarsnap peas or spinach all work well.

Fresh.text: 1/2 fresh red chilli, deseeded and finely sliced
1 red pepper, deseeded and finely sliced

Pantry.text: 1 tin (400 g) chickpeas, drained and rinsed
A handful of toasted nuts for garnish

Condiments.text: 1 tbsp vegetable oil (e.g. rapeseed, sunflower, canola)

steps.text: Heat {vegetable oil} in a large pan over high heat. Once hot, add {garlic}, {ginger}, {red chilli} and {spring onions}. Cook for one and a half minutes, stirring regularly.

durations.text: for one and a half minutes

steps.text: Add {courgette} and {red pepper}. Cook for one minute, then add {tamari} and cook for another minute. Reduce heat to medium.
In a blender or bowl, combine {peanut butter}, {apple cider vinegar}, {maple syrup} and half the {water}. Blend or whisk until smooth, then add to the pan.
Pour in the remaining water along with the {chickpeas}. Turn the heat back up to high and bring to the boil, then remove from the heat.
Season with {salt} and {black pepper} to taste.
Divide between four bowls and top with {coriander}, {basil}, {bean sprouts} and {toasted nuts}.

name: One-Pot Creamy Mushroom Pasta
description: A quick one-pot wonder where spaghetti cooks directly in the sauce for a creamy, cheesy result — no cream or cheese required
notes: If you don't want to use wine, replace it with the same amount of vegetable stock.

Fridge.text: 800 ml oat milk (or other non-dairy milk)

Pantry.text: 2 tbsp nutritional yeast, plus extra for garnish

Condiments.text: 2 tbsp vegetable oil (e.g. rapeseed, sunflower, canola)

steps.text: Finely chop {red onion} and {garlic}. Chop {mushrooms} reasonably finely.

Heat {vegetable oil} in a large pan over high heat. Add {red onion} and {garlic} and cook until they start to brown, about two to three minutes, stirring continuously. Add {mushrooms} and cook for two minutes.
Add {white wine} and most of the {thyme} leaves, and cook for another minute.

Break {spaghetti} into the pan and add {oat milk}, {vegetable stock} and {salt}. Turn the heat up high and cover with a lid. Once boiling, remove the lid to let the liquid evaporate. Stir occasionally to prevent sticking. When the sauce has reduced and the pasta is cooked, stir in {nutritional yeast} and a squeeze of {lemon} juice.
Season with {black pepper} and more salt if needed. Top each serving with the reserved {thyme} sprigs, extra nutritional yeast and {chilli flakes} if desired.

serving_suggestions: Serve straight from the pan. A green salad on the side keeps it fresh and light.

name: One-Pot Spag Bol
description: A quick vegan take on spaghetti bolognese with lentils — pasta and sauce cooked together in one pot for a rich, satisfying result
notes: Nutritional yeast has a savoury, cheesy flavour and is available in most health food stores and online. Use tamari instead of soy sauce to make this gluten-free.

Fresh.text: A handful of cherry tomatoes, halved

Pantry.text: 1 tin (400 g) lentils, drained and rinsed

steps.text: Finely chop {red onion} and {garlic}. Grate {carrot}, thinly slice {celery} and finely chop {chilli}. Halve {cherry tomatoes}. Drain and rinse {lentils}.
Heat {olive oil} in a large pot over high heat. Add {red onion}, {garlic}, {carrot}, {celery}, {chilli} and a pinch of {salt}. Cook for three minutes, stirring regularly.

Add {chopped tomatoes}, {vegetable stock}, {spaghetti} (break it to fit if needed -- an Italian panda might die though, so you might have it on your conscience), {tamari} and {cherry tomatoes}. Bring to the boil, then simmer until the pasta is cooked, about nine to eleven minutes. Remove from the heat.
Pluck {basil} leaves and set aside. Finely chop the stalks and stir into the pot. Season with {black pepper} and a squeeze of {lemon} juice.
Serve topped with the basil leaves and {nutritional yeast}.

name: Chickpea Tikka Masala
description: Chickpeas simmered in a creamy, spiced tomato and coconut milk sauce
notes: US recipes often call this "tomato puree" — in European terms that's passata (sieved tomatoes), not the concentrated paste. A standard 400 g carton works perfectly. Ginger paste from a tube is fine here, or grate a small thumb of fresh ginger.

Pantry.text: 2 tins (400 g) chickpeas, drained and rinsed

steps.text: Heat {olive oil} in a large saucepan over medium heat. Cook {onion} until softened, about three to four minutes.

Add {garlic} and {ginger paste}, saute for one minute until fragrant. Stir in {garam masala}, {ground cumin}, {turmeric}, {ground coriander}, and {black pepper}. Fry for thirty seconds, stirring constantly.

Pour in {passata}, {water}, {chickpeas}, and {salt}. Bring to a rapid simmer, then reduce to medium-low. Simmer covered for twenty minutes, stirring occasionally, until the sauce thickens and darkens.
Stir in {coconut milk} and {brown sugar}. Simmer for a further two to three minutes.
Taste and adjust seasoning. Garnish with {coriander}.

serving_suggestions: Serve with basmati rice and naan. A dollop of coconut yoghurt on top works well.

name: Persian Celery Stew (Khoresht-e-Karafs)
description: A fragrant Iranian stew with celery, butter beans, fried herbs, saffron, and lime — traditionally served over steamed basmati rice.
notes: A dish from northern Iran where butter beans are a common plant-based swap for the traditional lamb. Don't rush the herb frying step — the parsley and mint need to darken and concentrate to develop the characteristic deep flavour. The stew should have some liquid remaining when done; don't let it dry out.

Fresh.text: 6-8 stalks celery, cut into 3 cm pieces

Pantry.text: 1 tin (400 g) butter beans, drained and rinsed

Condiments.text: 5 tbsp vegetable oil (e.g. rapeseed, sunflower, canola)

steps.text: Dissolve {saffron} in 60 ml boiling water and set aside to bloom.
Heat 3 tbsp {vegetable oil} in a frying pan over medium heat. Add {parsley} and {mint} and fry for ten to fifteen minutes, stirring often, until darkened and fragrant. Set aside.
Heat the remaining {vegetable oil} in a large pot over medium heat. Add {onions} and cook for about ten minutes until translucent.
Add {ground turmeric} and {garlic}, stir for one minute, then add {celery} and cook for another five minutes.
Add {butter beans} and the fried herbs. Stir together for one minute.
Pour in 700 ml boiling water, then add the {vegetable stock} cube, {lime juice}, and the bloomed saffron water. Stir to combine.
Cover and simmer on low heat for forty-five to sixty minutes, until the celery is tender. The stew should retain some liquid.
Taste and season with {salt} and {black pepper}.

serving_suggestions: Serve hot over steamed basmati rice. A dollop of vegan yoghurt on the side works well.

name: Dal Tadka
description: Classic hearty lentil curry with a tempered spice topping
notes: Red lentils break down into a creamy texture, yellow lentils hold their shape a bit more — both work well. Lentils are easy to find in any German supermarket or Asian shop.

Condiments.text: 1 tbsp vegetable oil (e.g. rapeseed, sunflower, canola)

steps.text: Rinse {lentils} and cook in water until soft, about fifteen minutes. Drain any excess water.
In a pan, heat {vegetable oil} and fry the {cumin seeds} until they start to pop, then add {onion} and cook until golden.
Add {garlic}, {ginger}, {turmeric}, {garam masala} and {chilli}. Cook for two minutes, stirring often.
Add the {chopped tomatoes} and cook for five minutes.
Stir in the cooked lentils and simmer together for ten minutes.
Season with {salt} and finish with a squeeze of {lemon} juice.

serving_suggestions: Serve with basmati rice or naan bread. A dollop of coconut yoghurt and fresh coriander on top works nicely.

name: Palak Tofu
description: Vegan saag paneer — crispy tofu in a creamy spiced spinach sauce
notes: Press the tofu well — at least fifteen minutes with a cloth or kitchen paper — for crispier results. Fresh spinach gives a brighter colour but frozen works fine if well drained.

Condiments.text: 2 tbsp vegetable oil (e.g. rapeseed, sunflower, canola)

steps.text: Press {tofu} with a cloth or kitchen paper for at least fifteen minutes to remove excess moisture, then cut into 2 cm cubes.
Heat {vegetable oil} in a pan over high heat. Pan-fry the tofu until golden and crispy on all sides. Set aside.
In the same pan, fry the {cumin seeds} until they pop, then add {onions} and cook until deeply golden, about ten minutes.
Add {garlic} and {ginger}, cook for two minutes, then add {turmeric}, {ground coriander} and {chilli flakes}. Stir well.
Add the {spinach} and let it wilt completely.
Blend everything into a smooth sauce using a hand blender.
Stir in the {coconut cream} and simmer for five minutes.
Add the tofu back in, season with {garam masala}, {nutmeg} and {salt}, and simmer for another five minutes. Finish with a squeeze of {lemon} juice.

serving_suggestions: Serve with basmati rice or naan. A squeeze of lemon at the end brightens it up nicely.

name: Rajma
description: Rich red kidney bean curry — even better the next day
notes: Don't rush the onions — cooking them until deep golden brown is key to the richness of the dish. Like most curries, this one is even better the next day once the flavours have had time to develop.

Pantry.text: 2 tins (400 g each) kidney beans, drained and rinsed

Condiments.text: 1 tbsp vegetable oil (e.g. rapeseed, sunflower, canola)

steps.text: Heat {vegetable oil} in a heavy-bottomed pan and fry the {cumin seeds} until they pop.
Add the {onions} and cook on medium heat for twelve to fifteen minutes until deep golden brown.
Add {garlic} and {ginger}, cook for two minutes.
Add {turmeric}, {ground coriander}, {smoked paprika}, {chilli powder} and {tomato puree}. Stir well and cook for two minutes.
Add the {chopped tomatoes} and cook for eight to ten minutes until the sauce thickens and the oil starts to separate slightly.
Add the {kidney beans} and {vegetable stock}, stir well, cover and simmer on low heat for twenty to twenty-five minutes.
Lightly mash a few beans with the back of a spoon to thicken the sauce further.
Season with {salt}, finish with {garam masala} and fresh {coriander}.

serving_suggestions: Serve with basmati rice. A squeeze of lemon and a dollop of coconut yoghurt work well on top.

name: Teriyaki Soba Noodles
description: Quick stir-fried soba noodles with mushrooms, bok choy and teriyaki sauce
notes: This comes together in twenty minutes — prep the vegetables while the water boils. If the noodles start sticking after draining, rinse them with cold water. Add teriyaki sauce gradually to taste — different brands vary in sweetness and saltiness.

Fresh.text: 3 bunches bok choy, chopped, whites and greens separated
1 bunch spring onions, chopped, whites and greens separated

Condiments.text: 80 ml teriyaki sauce, or to taste

steps.text: Bring a pot of water to a boil. While waiting, prep the vegetables. Add {soba noodles} and cook for three to five minutes until tender. Drain and rinse with cold water.

Meanwhile, heat {sesame oil} in a skillet over medium heat. Add {garlic}, {mushrooms}, whites of the {bok choy}, whites of the {spring onion}, {white pepper} and {chilli flakes}. Cook for about five minutes until the mushrooms have released their liquid and it has cooked off.
Add 2 tablespoons of {teriyaki sauce} and cook for one more minute.
Add the soba noodles, greens of the {bok choy} and the rest of the {teriyaki sauce}. Toss until everything is coated and heated through.
Serve topped with remaining {spring onions} and {sesame seeds}.

name: Galettes Bretonnes
description: Traditional Breton buckwheat crêpes with sautéed mushrooms — naturally vegan and gluten-free
notes: The batter needs at least 2 hours of resting in the fridge, ideally overnight — plan ahead. The first galette is almost always a throwaway (la galette de test!) so don't worry about it. Good buckwheat flour is key — look for farine de sarrasin or blé noir. Stack cooked galettes under a tea towel to keep them warm and pliable. Leftovers keep well in the fridge for 2–3 days; reheat in a dry pan.

Fresh.text: 200 g mushrooms (chestnut, oyster, or shiitake), sliced

Condiments.text: 1 tbsp vegetable oil (e.g. rapeseed, sunflower, canola)

steps.text: Sift the {buckwheat flour} into a large bowl and add the {salt}. Gradually pour in the {water} while whisking continuously until the batter is smooth and thin, like single cream. Cover and rest in the fridge for at least 2 hours, ideally overnight.
When ready to cook, check the batter consistency and add a splash of water if needed.

Heat a drizzle of {vegetable oil} in a pan over medium heat. Add the {mushrooms} and cook for five minutes until golden. Add the {garlic} and {thyme}, cook for another minute, then add the {soy sauce}. Stir and set aside.
Heat a large flat pan over medium-high heat. Lightly grease with {vegetable oil}.
Pour a ladleful of batter and quickly spread it as thinly as possible.
Cook for about two minutes until the edges lift and turn crispy. Flip and cook for one more minute.
Spoon the mushroom filling onto the galette, fold the edges in, sprinkle with fresh {parsley}, and serve immediately.

serving_suggestions:
Serve with a bowl of cidre breton for the full Breton experience. Other vegan filling ideas: creamy leeks with oat cream and nutmeg; ratatouille; caramelised onion and spinach with vegan cheese; roasted butternut squash with cumin, hummus, and toasted pumpkin seeds; or vegan pesto with sun-dried tomatoes, pine nuts, and rocket.

For vegetarian fillings: a fried egg with grated Comté (the classic complète); goat cheese with honey and walnuts; sautéed mushrooms with egg and Gruyère; or wilted spinach with ricotta, lemon zest, and black pepper.

name: Sesame Tantanmen Ramen
description: Creamy sesame broth with spicy tofu crumbles and fresh vegetables
notes: Tantanmen is the Japanese take on Sichuan dan dan noodles. The key is the sesame paste — use Asian sesame paste (not tahini), which is made from toasted seeds and has a much deeper flavour. If you can't find rayu (Japanese chilli oil), any chilli oil works. Press the tofu well before crumbling for crispier results.

steps.text: Heat a drizzle of {sesame oil} in a skillet over medium heat. Add {garlic} and {ginger}, stir for thirty seconds until fragrant.
Add the crumbled {tofu}, {soy sauce} (1/2 tbsp), {miso paste}, and {chilli oil}. Fry for three to five minutes, stirring often, until the tofu is golden and crispy. Set aside.
Steam or blanch the {cabbage} and {bean sprouts} for about three minutes until just tender. Set aside.
In a small bowl, mix the {Asian sesame paste}, remaining {soy sauce} (1 1/2 tbsp), {rice vinegar}, 1 tsp {sesame oil}, and {rayu} into a smooth paste.
In a saucepan, heat the {soy milk} and {vegetable stock} over medium-low heat. When it starts to bubble, reduce the heat to low and whisk in the sesame paste mixture until fully dissolved.
Cook the {ramen noodles} according to the package instructions. Drain and divide between two bowls.
Pour the hot broth over the noodles. Top with the spicy tofu crumbles, steamed vegetables, {sweetcorn}, {spring onions}, {peanuts}, and {sesame seeds}. Finish with extra {rayu} to taste.

serving_suggestions: Serve immediately while piping hot. For extra heat, add more rayu or a drizzle of chilli oil on top. A squeeze of lime on the side is a nice addition. If you want a richer broth, stir in an extra tablespoon of sesame paste.

name: Tofu Scramble
description: The perfect egg-free scramble — spiced with kala namak and chipotle, creamy on the inside, crispy on the outside
notes: Press the tofu for at least 24 hours to extract all the water. The drier the tofu, the better it will brown. Kala namak (Indian black salt) is the secret ingredient — it gives the scramble its eggy flavour. You can find it at Indian grocery stores or online.

Fridge.text: 120 ml oat milk (or creamy plant milk)

steps.text: Make the eggy sauce. In a bowl, whisk together the {ground turmeric}, {garlic powder}, {onion powder}, {paprika}, {chipotle chile flakes}, {kala namak}, {tahini}, and {nutritional yeast}. Gradually pour in the {oat milk} and whisk until smooth.
Crumble the pressed {tofu} with your hands into chunks — not too big, not too small.

Heat a large nonstick frying pan over medium-high heat with the {olive oil}. Once hot, add the tofu and fry until lightly browned, five to seven minutes. Stir occasionally but not too often to let the exterior crisp up.
Pour in the eggy sauce and continue frying until the scramble is as moist or dry as you like — pull it off the heat early for a soft, creamy scramble, or keep going for firmer, drier curds.
For extra creaminess, stir in the {vegan yogurt} and mix through.
Taste and add a pinch {kala namak} and {black pepper} if needed.

serving_suggestions: Serve with halved cherry tomatoes, sliced cucumber, bell pepper strips, and toasted crusty bread.

name: Chilli Sin Carne
description: A hearty vegan chilli packed with beans, lentils, and spices that tastes even better the next day
notes: The cocoa powder adds a lovely depth without making it taste chocolatey. Add the chilli powder gradually and taste as you go if you're not sure about heat levels. For a smoky twist, add a finely chopped chipotle pepper or a teaspoon of chipotle paste. Freezes brilliantly in portions for easy weeknight dinners.

Pantry.text: 1 tin (400 g) kidney beans, drained and rinsed
1 tin (400 g) black beans, drained and rinsed

steps.text: Heat {olive oil} in a large pot over medium heat. Add the {onion} and cook for about five minutes until softened.
Add the {garlic}, {red pepper}, {yellow pepper}, {carrots}, and {celery}. Cook for another five minutes, stirring occasionally.
Stir in the {ground cumin}, {smoked paprika}, {chilli powder}, {ground coriander}, {oregano}, and {cayenne pepper}. Cook for one minute to toast the spices.
Add the {tomato puree} and stir well.
Pour in the {chopped tomatoes} and {vegetable stock}. Add the {kidney beans}, {black beans}, {red lentils}, and {sweetcorn}.
Bring to a boil, then reduce to a low simmer. Cover and cook for twenty-five to thirty minutes, stirring occasionally, until the lentils are soft and the chilli has thickened.
Stir in the {cocoa powder}, squeeze in the {lime} juice, and season with {salt} and {pepper}.
Taste and adjust the spices as needed. Let it sit for a few minutes before serving.

serving_suggestions: Serve over rice, with baked potatoes, or with tortilla chips and guacamole. Top with vegan sour cream, fresh coriander, diced avocado, sliced spring onions, or a squeeze of lime.

name: Kale and White Bean Stew
description: A hearty, comforting stew with smoky spices, tender kale, and creamy white beans
notes: This stew actually tastes even better the next day as the flavours develop. Keeps in the fridge for 3 to 4 days and freezes well in portions for easy future meals.

Fresh.text: 120 g kale, stems removed and leaves roughly chopped
Fresh.preparation: stems removed and leaves roughly chopped

Pantry.text: 1 tin (400 g) white beans (cannellini or butter beans), drained and rinsed
Pantry.preparation: (cannellini or butter beans), drained and rinsed

steps.text: Heat the {olive oil} in a large pot over medium heat. Add the {onion}, {carrot}, and {celery}, and cook for about five to seven minutes until softened.

durations.text: for about five to seven minutes

steps.text: Add the {garlic}, {smoked paprika}, {ground cumin}, and {chilli flakes}. Stir for about a minute until fragrant.
Pour in the {chopped tomatoes} and {vegetable stock}. Give it a good stir and bring to a simmer.
Add the {white beans} and let it simmer for about ten minutes so the flavours come together.
Toss in the {kale} and stir it through. Let it cook for another five to seven minutes until the kale is tender but still has a bit of bite.
Season with {salt}, {pepper}, and the {lemon} juice. Taste and adjust as needed.

serving_suggestions: Serve with a chunk of crusty bread for dipping. Drizzle with good olive oil and a sprinkle of nutritional yeast for a savoury, cheesy flavour.

name: French Crêpes
description: A proper French recipe, no eggs, no dairy, all delicious
notes: The resting time is important. It lets the flour fully hydrate and the gluten relax, which makes the crêpes easier to spread thinly and less likely to tear. The first crêpe is almost always a disaster. Consider it a test run and adjust the heat accordingly. La première est pour le chien! Corn starch will give slightly crispier crêpes, potato starch will give softer, more flexible ones.

Fridge.text: A little vegan butter for the pan

Pantry.text: 30 g corn starch or potato starch

Condiments.text: 2 tbsp vegetable oil (e.g. rapeseed, sunflower, canola)
1 tbsp vanilla extract (optional, for sweet crêpes)
1 tbsp orange blossom water (optional, for sweet crêpes)

steps.text: Sift the {plain flour} and {corn starch} into a large bowl. Add the {sugar} and {salt}.
Make a well in the centre and gradually pour in the {oat milk} while whisking from the centre outward, incorporating the flour slowly. This avoids lumps.
Add the {vegetable oil} and {orange blossom water} (or {vanilla extract}) and whisk until smooth.
Add the {sparkling water} and give it a final mix.
Let the batter rest for an hour. It should have the consistency of liquid cream. If it's too thick after resting, add a splash of oat milk or water to thin it out.
Heat a crêpe pan (or flat non-stick pan) on medium-high heat. Melt a small knob of {vegan butter} on the pan.
Pour a thin layer of batter, swirling quickly to cover the whole surface.
Cook for about one to two minutes per side until lightly golden.

durations.text: for about one to two minutes

steps.text: Repeat, adding a little {vegan butter} to the pan between crêpes.

serving_suggestions: Spread with Nutella (ideally a version without palm oil), lemon and sugar, or fresh fruit and whipped coconut cream. For savoury crêpes, skip the sugar and fill with sautéed mushrooms, spinach, or ratatouille.

name: Creamy One Pan Spinach Gnocchi
description: Quick and creamy one-pan gnocchi in a coconut cream sauce with sun-dried tomatoes, garlic, and spinach — completely vegan and ready in 20 minutes

Fridge.text: 2 tbsp cream cheese, vegan or regular

Pantry.text: 55 g sun-dried tomatoes, finely chopped (oil packed)
2 tbsp nutritional yeast (or finely grated parmesan)

steps.text: Warm {olive oil} and {vegan butter} in a wide skillet over medium heat. Add {onion} and {garlic}, cook for two to three minutes until fragrant.

Pour in {coconut cream} and whisk in {cream cheese} until smooth. Simmer two to three minutes to gently reduce. Stir in {sun-dried tomatoes}, {nutritional yeast}, {Italian seasoning}, {black pepper}, and a pinch of {salt}.

Add {gnocchi} and stir well. Cover and cook three to four minutes, stirring once or twice, until tender and the sauce is silky. If the sauce tightens too much, add splashes of {water} as needed. If it's too loose, let it bubble uncovered to thicken.
Stir in {spinach} until wilted. Mix in {lemon} juice if using. Taste and adjust seasoning.

serving_suggestions: Serve with a drizzle of oil from the sun-dried tomatoes.

name: Classic British Scones
description: Light and fluffy vegan scones perfect for afternoon tea
notes: Keep the margarine cold – this helps create a flaky texture. Don't overwork the dough, just bring it together gently. Best eaten fresh on the day, but they freeze well too. Just defrost at room temperature and reheat in a hot oven for a few minutes.

Fridge.text: 150 ml plant milk, plus a splash for brushing

steps.text: Preheat the oven to 220°C / 200°C fan / Gas 7 and lightly grease a large baking tray.
Whisk together {plain flour}, {baking powder}, and {salt} so the raising agent is evenly distributed.
Using your fingertips, rub the cold {margarine} into the {plain flour} until the mixture resembles fine breadcrumbs.
Stir in {caster sugar}. Add most of the {plant milk} and mix to form a soft and slightly sticky dough. If it's a little dry, add more until you get the right consistency.
Turn out onto a floured work surface and knead very lightly. Pat out into a round about 2cm thick.
Use a 5cm cutter to stamp out rounds and place on the baking tray. Lightly knead together any remaining dough and stamp out more scones to use it all up.
Brush the tops with the remaining {plant milk}.
Bake for twelve to fifteen minutes until well risen and golden.
Cool on a wire rack. Serve with jam and vegan butter, or vegan whipped cream if you're feeling fancy!

serving_suggestions: Serve warm with jam and vegan butter. For a traditional cream tea, add vegan whipped cream. Try adding dried fruit or a pinch of cinnamon to the dough for a twist.

name: Silky Smooth Hummus
description: Silky smooth hummus with the perfect creamy texture
notes: Use warm chickpeas, as they're softer and easier to blend. The ice cubes are not optional! The cold shock against the warm chickpeas aerates the hummus beautifully. Don't add olive oil to the base mixture. The tahini has plenty of fat. Olive oil goes on top when serving. The hummus keeps well in the fridge for up to 3 days.

Spices.text: Pinch of ground cumin (optional, for topping)

steps.text: Cover {chickpeas} with plenty of water, add half the {baking soda}, and leave overnight.

Drain and rinse the {chickpeas}. Place them in a pan with the other half of the {baking soda} and stir over medium heat for a few minutes. Add enough water to cover them by a few centimeters, bring to a boil, and simmer for twenty to forty minutes until they're really soft with no bite at all. Skim off any foam and loose skins as they cook.
Drain {chickpeas} over a bowl, saving the cooking water.
While the {chickpeas} are still warm, put them into a food processor with {tahini}, {garlic}, {lemon} juice, {ice} cubes, 2 tablespoons of the reserved chickpea water, and a good pinch of {salt}.

Blitz until smooth, then taste and adjust. You might need more {tahini}, {garlic}, {lemon}, {salt}, or chickpea water. Don't be shy about adding more {tahini}. Blitz for a few minutes at least until it's really smooth and creamy.
Transfer to a bowl, cover the surface with cling film, and let it rest for at least thirty minutes.
Serve drizzled with good {olive oil} and a squeeze of {lemon}. Optionally sprinkle with {ground cumin}, {paprika}, {sumac}, or {za'atar} for extra flavour.

serving_suggestions: Drizzle with your best olive oil and add a squeeze of fresh lemon. Sprinkle with cumin, paprika, sumac, or za'atar. Serve with warm pita bread, fresh vegetables, or use as a sandwich spread. For the tahini, Al Arz is a great brand that's not too bitter.

name: Creamy Mushroom Soup
description: Rich and creamy mushroom soup perfect for batch cooking and freezing
notes: For a deeper, more umami flavour, add a splash of dry white wine after cooking the onions and let it reduce before adding the stock. If you have dried porcini, soak a small handful in warm water for twenty minutes, chop them up and add them along with their soaking liquid for incredible depth. The soup keeps well in the fridge for 3-4 days and also freezes beautifully.

Fresh.text: 500 g mushrooms (champignon, king oyster, shiitake), sliced
1 medium potato (about 150 g), peeled and cubed

steps.text: Heat {olive oil} in a large pot over medium-high heat. Add {mushrooms} and cook for about eight minutes until they've released their liquid and turned golden brown. Set aside a small handful for garnish if you like.
Lower the heat to medium, add a little more oil if needed, and cook {onion} for about five minutes until soft and translucent.
Add {garlic} and {thyme}, stir for about a minute until fragrant.
Add {potato} cubes and {vegetable stock}. Bring to a boil, then reduce to a simmer and cook for about fifteen minutes, until the potato is completely tender.
Stir in {oat cream} and {soy sauce}.
Blend the soup using a stick blender until smooth and creamy. If you set some mushrooms aside earlier, stir them back in now for a bit of texture.
Season with {salt}, {pepper}, and a squeeze of {lemon} juice. The lemon really lifts the flavour.
Serve with fresh {parsley} on top and some crusty bread on the side.

serving_suggestions: Serve with crusty bread for dipping. Add a swirl of extra oat cream and a drizzle of truffle oil for a fancy touch. Great with a side salad for a complete meal.

name: Pain d'Épice
description: Traditional French spiced honey cake with warming spices
notes: This delicious French spice bread is best enjoyed the next day when the flavours have had time to develop. It keeps well wrapped in foil for up to a week. The spice mix typically includes cinnamon, green aniseed, star anise, nutmeg, coriander seeds, cloves and ground ginger, but you can also use a pre-made pain d'épices spice blend if available.

Fridge.text: 250 ml (1 cup) milk (or plant milk for a vegan version)
Fridge.preparation: (or plant milk for a vegan version)

Pantry.text: 250 g (1-3/4 cups) plain flour (T45 / Type 405 / Tipo 00)

Condiments.text: 3 tbsp honey (or jam for a vegan version)

Spices.text: 2 tsp spice mix (cinnamon, green aniseed, star anise, grated nutmeg, coriander seeds, cloves, ground ginger)
Spices.preparation: (cinnamon, green aniseed, star anise, grated nutmeg, coriander seeds, cloves, ground ginger)

steps.text: Preheat the oven to 180°C (160°C fan).
In a large bowl, mix together {brown sugar}, {plain flour}, {honey}, {baking soda}, {orange blossom water}, {spice mix}, and {milk} until well combined and smooth.
Pour the batter into a greased rectangular baking tin (about 20cm x 10cm).
Bake for forty-five minutes, or until a skewer inserted into the center comes out clean.
Let cool in the tin for ten minutes, then turn out onto a wire rack to cool completely.

serving_suggestions: Slice and enjoy with butter, or serve with tea or coffee. Even better the next day when the flavours have mellowed and the texture becomes more moist. Store wrapped in foil to keep fresh.

name: Creamy Pumpkin & Potato Curry
description: Warming vegan curry perfect for batch cooking and meal prep
notes: If you like a bit of heat, add a chopped chilli or some chilli flakes in step 2. A handful of spinach stirred in at the end is a nice addition. Keeps well in the fridge for 3–4 days and actually tastes even better the next day!

Condiments.text: 1 tbsp vegetable oil (e.g. rapeseed, sunflower, canola)

steps.text: Heat {vegetable oil} in a large pot over medium heat. Add {onion} and cook for about five minutes until soft.
Add {garlic}, {ginger}, {curry paste}, {ground cumin}, and {ground turmeric}. Stir and cook for another minute until fragrant.
Add {pumpkin} and {potato} cubes and stir everything together so they're coated in the spices.
Pour in {coconut milk} and {vegetable stock}. Give it a good stir and bring to a gentle boil.

Reduce the heat, cover, and let it simmer for about twenty to twenty-five minutes, stirring occasionally, until both the pumpkin and potatoes are tender. The floury potatoes will start to break down and thicken the sauce beautifully.

durations.text: for about twenty to twenty-five minutes

steps.text: Season with {salt} and {pepper}, then serve over rice (or with naan) topped with fresh {coriander} and a squeeze of {lime}.

serving_suggestions: Serve over basmati rice or with warm naan bread. Top with fresh coriander and a squeeze of lime. Great for meal prep – portion into containers for easy weeknight dinners throughout the week.

name: Banana Bread
description: Moist and delicious banana bread made with simple plant-based ingredients
notes: Use very ripe bananas with lots of brown spots for the best flavour and natural sweetness. The banana bread keeps well wrapped in foil for 3-4 days at room temperature, or can be frozen for up to 3 months. For extra indulgence, fold in some chocolate chips or chopped walnuts before baking.

Fresh.text: 4-5 medium bananas, mashed, saving a few slices for topping
Fresh.preparation: mashed, saving a few slices for topping

Condiments.text: 60 ml vegetable oil (e.g. rapeseed, sunflower, canola)

steps.text: Preheat the oven to 180°C (160°C fan). Spray a 9×5 inch (23×13cm) loaf pan with nonstick spray, then dust with flour.
In a large mixing bowl, roughly mash most of the {bananas}. Add {brown sugar}, {vegetable oil}, {vanilla extract}, and {plant milk}, and whisk to incorporate.
Sift in {plain flour}, {baking soda}, {salt}, and {cinnamon}, and use a wooden spoon to mix until just combined. Don't overmix.
Transfer the batter to your prepared pan, and top with the reserved {banana} slices.
Bake for about forty-five minutes, then cover with foil and bake for ten to fifteen minutes longer, until a toothpick inserted in the center comes out with just a couple of crumbs.
Let cool in the pan for ten minutes, then turn out onto a wire rack to cool completely before slicing.

serving_suggestions: Enjoy warm with vegan butter, or toasted the next day. Delicious for breakfast with coffee or as an afternoon snack. Try spreading with peanut butter or almond butter for extra protein.
